description.task: Solve the N-Queens problem using backtracking. Place N queens on an N×N chessboard such that no two queens attack each other. Print all solutions for N=4.
instructions: In the for loop: if(isSafe(board,row,col)){ board[row]=col; solve(board,row+1,n); }
No explicit undo needed because board[row] is overwritten on the next iteration
isSafe checks same column and both diagonals against all previously placed queens
For N=4 there are exactly 2 solutions: [1,3,0,2] and [2,0,3,1]
board[r] = column index of queen in row r
description.expectedOutput:
Solution 1: [1, 3, 0, 2]
Solution 2: [2, 0, 3, 1]
Total solutions for N=4: 2

description.task: Implement a Sudoku solver using backtracking. Given a partially filled 9×9 grid (0 = empty), fill in digits 1-9 so every row, column, and 3×3 box contains each digit exactly once.
instructions: After trying all digits 1-9 with no solution, return false to trigger backtracking in caller
isValid checks the entire row, column, and 3×3 box in a single loop using i/3 and i%3 offsets
This is a classic constraint satisfaction problem solved via backtracking
Expected output is the completed 9×9 grid for the given puzzle

description.task: Generate all permutations of a given string using backtracking. Swap characters to build permutations in-place. Print every unique permutation of 'ABC'.
instructions: Inside the for loop: swap(arr,start,i); permute(arr,start+1); swap(arr,start,i);
The third swap restores the array — this is the backtracking step
For a 3-character string ABC, there are 3! = 6 permutations
After sorting, output order: ABC, ACB, BAC, BCA, CAB, CBA
This pattern generalises to any array of distinct elements
description.expectedOutput:
ABC
ACB
BAC
BCA
CAB
CBA
Total permutations: 6
